questions.stem: A map $T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ is linear if it satisfies which two conditions for all vectors $u, v$ and scalars $c$?
questions.explanation: Linearity means $T$ preserves addition, $T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v)$, and preserves scalar multiplication, $T(cu) = cT(u)$. Both must hold. The option giving only additivity is incomplete, since a map can be additive without respecting scalar multiplication. Multiplicative conditions like $T(uv) = T(u)T(v)$ describe ring homomorphisms, not linear maps.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: For a linear transformation $T$, what is $T(\mathbf{0})$?
questions.explanation: Every linear transformation sends the zero vector to the zero vector. Using $T(\mathbf{0}) = T(0 \cdot v) = 0 \cdot T(v) = \mathbf{0}$. This is a quick test: if a map sends $\mathbf{0}$ anywhere other than $\mathbf{0}$, it cannot be linear. The trap option calling it undefined ignores that $T$ is defined on the whole domain, including $\mathbf{0}$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Is $T(x, y) = (x + 1, y)$ a linear transformation on $\mathbb{R}^2$?
options: Yes, because it is a translation
questions.explanation: Check the zero vector: $T(0, 0) = (0 + 1, 0) = (1, 0) \neq (0, 0)$. Since a linear map must fix the origin, $T$ is not linear. The extra $+1$ is a translation, which is affine but not linear. The trap option calling it linear because it is a translation confuses affine maps with linear ones.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: What is the standard matrix of the linear transformation $T(x, y) = (2x + y, \ x - 3y)$?
questions.explanation: The standard matrix has columns $T(e_1)$ and $T(e_2)$. Here $T(1, 0) = (2, 1)$ and $T(0, 1) = (1, -3)$, so the matrix is $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$. Equivalently, read the coefficients of the output rows directly. The trap option transposes the matrix, placing $T(e_1)$ and $T(e_2)$ in rows instead of columns.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: The standard matrix of $T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ is $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$. What does $T$ do?
questions.explanation: Since $A = 3I$, we have $T(v) = 3v$ for every vector $v$, so $T$ scales every vector by the factor $3$. A reflection would have a $-1$ on the diagonal, a rotation would mix the components with off-diagonal entries, and a projection would have a zero on the diagonal. Only uniform scaling matches $3I$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Find the kernel of the linear transformation with matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: The kernel solves $Av = \mathbf{0}$. Both rows give $x + 2y = 0$, so $x = -2y$; taking $y = -1$ gives $\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$, which spans the kernel. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$ spans the column space, not the kernel; the kernel direction is perpendicular to the row $(1, 2)$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: The matrix of $T$ is $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. What is the image (range) of $T$?
questions.explanation: The image is the span of the columns $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, which is the $x$-axis. The map projects every vector onto the $x$-axis. The trap option, the $y$-axis, would be the image of a matrix whose single nonzero column points up, which is not the case here.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: A linear transformation $T : \mathbb{R}^4 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ has rank $3$. What is its nullity?
questions.explanation: By the rank-nullity theorem, $\operatorname{rank}(T) + \operatorname{nullity}(T)$ equals the dimension of the domain, which is $4$. So $\operatorname{nullity}(T) = 4 - 3 = 1$. The codomain dimension $3$ caps the rank but does not enter the nullity formula. The trap option $0$ would require the rank to equal the domain dimension $4$, impossible since the codomain has dimension $3$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: When is a linear transformation $T : V \to W$ one-to-one?
options: when its image is all of $W$
questions.explanation: A linear transformation is one-to-one if and only if its kernel contains only the zero vector. If $T(u) = T(v)$ then $T(u - v) = \mathbf{0}$, so $u - v$ lies in the kernel; a trivial kernel forces $u = v$. Having image all of $W$ describes onto, a separate condition. So injectivity is exactly the trivial-kernel criterion.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: A linear transformation $T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ has a $3 \times 3$ matrix with $\det = 0$. Is $T$ onto?
options: No, because the rank is less than $3$
Yes, because the domain and codomain match
questions.explanation: A zero determinant means the columns are dependent, so the rank is at most $2$ and the image is a proper subspace of $\mathbb{R}^3$. Therefore $T$ does not cover all of $\mathbb{R}^3$ and is not onto. For a square matrix, onto and one-to-one are equivalent, and both fail when the determinant is zero. The trap claiming it is always onto ignores the rank deficiency.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: The rotation by $90^\circ$ counterclockwise in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has which standard matrix?
questions.explanation: Rotation by angle $\theta$ has matrix $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$. For $\theta = 90^\circ$, $\cos\theta = 0$ and $\sin\theta = 1$, giving $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Check: $e_1 = (1, 0) \mapsto (0, 1)$, which is the correct counterclockwise image. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ is the clockwise rotation.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Compute $T(2, -1)$ where $T(x, y) = (x - y, \ 3x + 2y)$.
questions.explanation: Substitute $x = 2$, $y = -1$. First component: $x - y = 2 - (-1) = 3$. Second component: $3x + 2y = 3(2) + 2(-1) = 6 - 2 = 4$. So $T(2, -1) = (3, 4)$. The trap option $(1, 4)$ slips on the first component by computing $2 + (-1) = 1$ instead of $2 - (-1) = 3$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: A linear transformation $T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ can be:
questions.explanation: The image of $T$ lives in $\mathbb{R}^3$ but has dimension at most $\dim \mathbb{R}^2 = 2$, so it can never fill the $3$-dimensional codomain; thus $T$ is never onto. However, with rank $2$ the nullity is $0$ by rank-nullity, so $T$ can be one-to-one. The trap option claiming onto-but-not-one-to-one reverses what is possible when the codomain is larger.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: What is the standard matrix of the projection $T(x, y) = (x, 0)$ onto the $x$-axis?
questions.explanation: The columns are $T(e_1)$ and $T(e_2)$. Here $T(1, 0) = (1, 0)$ and $T(0, 1) = (0, 0)$, so the matrix is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ is the projection onto the $y$-axis, not the $x$-axis.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Find the rank of the linear transformation with matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Row reduction leaves a single nonzero row $(1, 2, 3)$, so there is one pivot and the rank is $1$. Equivalently, the column space is spanned by $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, since every column is a multiple of it. The trap option $3$ counts the columns rather than the independent rows.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: If $T : V \to V$ has matrix $A$ in one basis and $B$ in another, the two matrices are related by:
options: $B = P^{-1} A P$ for some invertible $P$
questions.explanation: Changing basis transforms the matrix of an operator by a similarity: $B = P^{-1} A P$, where $P$ is the change-of-basis matrix. This conjugation preserves eigenvalues, determinant, and trace. The trap option $B = P A P$ omits the inverse and is not generally a valid change of basis, since $P A P$ need not even agree with $A$ when $P = I$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Given the linear maps with $T(x, y) = (x + y, \ y)$ and $S(x, y) = (2x, \ x - y)$, what is $(S \circ T)(1, 1)$?
questions.explanation: First apply $T$: $T(1, 1) = (1 + 1, \ 1) = (2, 1)$. Then apply $S$ to $(2, 1)$: the first component is $2x = 2 \cdot 2 = 4$ and the second is $x - y = 2 - 1 = 1$, so $S(2, 1) = (4, 1)$. The trap option $(4, 0)$ mishandles the second component as $2 - 2$ instead of $2 - 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Is $T(x, y) = (x^2, \ y)$ a linear transformation on $\mathbb{R}^2$?
questions.explanation: Test scaling: $T(c \cdot (x, y)) = ((cx)^2, \ cy) = (c^2 x^2, \ cy)$, but $cT(x, y) = (c x^2, \ cy)$. These differ whenever $c^2 \neq c$, for example $c = 2$, so $T$ is not linear. The squaring breaks homogeneity. Although $T(\mathbf{0}) = \mathbf{0}$ holds, that alone does not make a map linear.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: A linear transformation $T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ has nullity $2$. What is the dimension of its image?
questions.explanation: By rank-nullity, $\operatorname{rank}(T) = \dim(\text{domain}) - \operatorname{nullity}(T) = 3 - 2 = 1$. The dimension of the image equals the rank, so it is $1$. The trap option $2$ reports the nullity itself rather than the rank.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: The standard matrix of $T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ has $T(e_1) = (1, 2)$ and $T(e_2) = (3, 4)$. What is $T(1, 1)$?
questions.explanation: By linearity, $T(1, 1) = T(e_1 + e_2) = T(e_1) + T(e_2) = (1, 2) + (3, 4) = (4, 6)$. Adding the images of the basis vectors gives the image of their sum. The trap option $(1, 2)$ returns only $T(e_1)$, forgetting the contribution of $e_2$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Test whether $T(x, y) = (3x, \ x + y)$ is linear, then build its standard matrix.
questions.solution: Checking the origin, $T(0, 0) = (0, 0)$, and one verifies additivity and scaling hold, so $T$ {{1}} linear. Its standard matrix has columns $T(e_1) = (3, 1)$ and $T(e_2) = (0, 1)$, so the top-left entry is {{2}} and the bottom-left entry is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Each output is a linear combination of $x$ and $y$ with no constant term, so $T$ is linear. The columns are $T(e_1) = (3, 1)$ and $T(e_2) = (0, 1)$, giving the matrix with top-left $3$ and bottom-left $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Find the kernel of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$ by solving $Av = \mathbf{0}$.
questions.solution: Both rows reduce to $x + 3y = 0$, so $x = -3y$. Taking $y = 1$ gives the first component $x =$ {{1}}, so the kernel is a line of dimension {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The two rows are proportional and reduce to $x + 3y = 0$, so $x = -3y$. With $y = 1$, the kernel is spanned by $(-3, 1)$, a single line of dimension $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Apply rank-nullity to a linear map $T : \mathbb{R}^5 \to \mathbb{R}^4$ with nullity $2$.
questions.solution: The domain has dimension {{1}}, so by rank-nullity the rank is $5 - 2 =$ {{2}}. Since the rank is less than $4$, the map {{3}} onto.
questions.explanation: The domain is $\mathbb{R}^5$, so rank $= 5 - 2 = 3$. The image has dimension $3 < 4$, so it cannot fill $\mathbb{R}^4$ and the map is not onto.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Compute $T(3, 2)$ for $T(x, y) = (x - 2y, \ 4x + y)$.
questions.solution: The first component is $x - 2y = 3 - 2 \cdot 2 =$ {{1}}. The second component is $4x + y = 4 \cdot 3 + 2 =$ {{2}}.
questions.explanation: First component: $3 - 4 = -1$. Second component: $12 + 2 = 14$. So $T(3, 2) = (-1, 14)$.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Decide whether $T(x, y) = (x + 2, \ y)$ is linear by testing the origin.
questions.solution: Evaluating at the origin gives $T(0, 0) = (2, 0)$, whose first component is {{1}}, not zero. Because a linear map must send the origin to the origin, $T$ {{2}} linear.
questions.explanation: Since $T(0,0) = (2, 0) \neq (0,0)$, the map fails the requirement that linear maps fix the origin. The constant $+2$ makes it affine, not linear.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Use the basis images to evaluate a linear map. Suppose $T(e_1) = (2, 1)$ and $T(e_2) = (-1, 3)$.
questions.solution: By linearity $T(1, 1) = T(e_1) + T(e_2)$, whose first component is $2 + (-1) =$ {{1}} and second component is $1 + 3 =$ {{2}}. More generally the standard matrix has $T(e_1)$ and $T(e_2)$ as its {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $T(1,1) = (2,1) + (-1,3) = (1, 4)$. The standard matrix is built by placing the images of the basis vectors as its columns.
questions.source: in the style of Friedberg, Linear Algebra
